triage_reasoning: EGFR (ErbB1/HER1) is a single-pass type I transmembrane glycoprotein with a large extracellular ligand-binding domain (domains I-IV, ~620 aa) facing the extracellular space, a single TM helix, and an intracellular kinase domain. It is constitutively expressed on the plasma membrane of epithelial and many other cell types. The extracellular domain is the target of approved therapeutic antibodies (cetuximab, panitumumab), ADCs (e.g., ABT-414/depatuxizumab mafodotin), and bispecifics — all engaging the cell-surface ectodomain on intact, non-permeabilized cells. Surface biotinylation and flow cytometry on intact cells unambiguously confirm stable PM residence. Ectodomain shedding by ADAM10/17 does occur, but the transmembrane precursor is the dominant stable form at the cell surface; the shed ectodomain is a secondary product. This is a canonical surface receptor, fully accessible from the extracellular face.

executive_summary.one_paragraph: EGFR is a canonical single-pass Type I transmembrane receptor with a large (~620 aa) extracellular domain constitutively displayed at the plasma membrane of epithelial and many tumor cell types. Multiple orthogonal direct-surface methods — live flow cytometry, non-permeabilized immunofluorescence, surface biotinylation–MS across 10+ independent cell models, and cryo-EM/crystal structures — unambiguously confirm stable plasma membrane residence. Approved therapeutic antibodies (cetuximab, panitumumab) engage the surface ECD on intact cells. Ectodomain shedding by ADAM10/17 occurs but is a minor secondary product; the membrane-anchored receptor dominates.

filters.expression_level_rationale: High-level surface EGFR confirmed by flow cytometry in glioma stem cells, pancreatic cancer, epidermoid carcinoma (A431), and primary SIP tumor tissue (97% membranous IHC); in top 84% of surfaceome expression in glandular tissue MS datasets.
filters.expression_breadth_rationale: Surface EGFR detected across epithelial (keratinocyte, cervical, pancreatic, lung, colorectal), neural (iPSC sensory neurons), glioma, prostate, and breast cancer contexts; pan-cancer N-glycoproteomics spans 7 tumor types (GBM, HNSC, LUSC, LUAD, PAAD, COAD, OV).
filters.surface_specificity_rationale: Primary compartment is plasma membrane (confirmed by live-cell methods), but EGF-induced internalization routes a fraction to endosomes and lysosomes. Non-permeabilized vs. permeabilized IF shows detectable intracellular pool; surface is dominant in resting cells.
filters.has_known_ligand_rationale: Multiple validated endogenous ligands including EGF, TGF-α, epiregulin, amphiregulin, betacellulin, HB-EGF, and epigen; EGF binding to domain III is well-characterized structurally (cryo-EM heart-shaped dimer) and competitively blocked by panitumumab.
filters.low_endogenous_expression_rationale: Derived from expression_level='high' (not low/absent → not flagged). High-level surface EGFR confirmed by flow cytometry in glioma stem cells, pancreatic cancer, epidermoid carcinoma (A431), and primary SIP tumor tissue (97% membranous IHC); in top 84% of surfaceome expression in glandular tissue MS datasets.
filters.overexpression_surface_localization_obse: No method observation pairs an overexpression/mixed expression system with a direct or supportive surface-accessibility readout.

surface_evidence.grade_rationale: EGFR achieves direct_multi_method: the ledger contains multiple independent direct surface methods spanning (1) live non-permeabilized flow cytometry across glioma (GSC83, U373VIII), pancreatic cancer (BxPC-3 and others), lung cancer (NCI-H1930), and epidermoid carcinoma (A431) cell lines — multiple high-weight supports from independent sources; (2) non-permeabilized vs. permeabilized immunofluorescence directly demonstrating surface vs. intracellular EGFR; and (3) cell-surface biotinylation + mass spectrometry or WB in keratinocytes (NIKS), DCDML cells, prostate cancer cells (sBioSITe, 19 biotinylation sites), iPSC-derived neurons (3 independent experiments), and polarized MDCK cells (apicobasal surfaceome with SILAC). Supporting structural data (cryo-EM, crystal structures) confirm canonical type I topology with an accessible ectodomain. IHC with explicit membranous staining criteria in primary SIP tumor tissue adds further support. No contradicting evidence exists — all claims are coherent. The breadth of direct surface methods (live flow, nonperm IF, surface biotinylation-MS) from multiple independent sources firmly anchors direct_multi_method.

therapeutic_engagement.description: EGFR is targeted by multiple approved clinical antibodies that bind its extracellular domain on the cell surface, including cetuximab (anti-EGFR IgG1, binds domain III, PDB: 1YY9), panitumumab (fully human anti-EGFR IgG2, KD ~5×10⁻¹¹ M, blocks EGF binding site; approved for colorectal cancer, Amgen), and matuzumab (PDB: 3C09). Preclinical/investigational programs include bispecific NK cell engagers incorporating panitumumab VH/VL sequences targeting EGFR on pancreatic cancer cells, toxicity-sparing bispecific antibodies against EGFR/FGFR1, and anti-EGFR affibody-polymer conjugates (528mAb). EGFR-targeted ADCs and CAR-T constructs also engage the surface ECD.
therapeutic_engagement.surface_form_rationale: EGFR is a single-pass type I transmembrane receptor; all therapeutic antibodies (cetuximab, panitumumab, matuzumab) engage the membrane-anchored extracellular domain (domains I–IV, residues 25–645) directly on the intact cell surface, as confirmed by crystal structures (PDB: 1YY9, 3C09, 7OM4), cryo-EM, flow cytometry on live non-permeabilized cells, and surface biotinylation assays. EGFR ligands (not EGFR itself) can be shed via ADAM17, but the receptor remains membrane-anchored.

cell_states: activated/phosphorylated EGFR (p-EGFR positive in 69% of cases)

cell_states.descriptor: EGF ligand binding triggers EGFR internalization and lysosomal degradation, reducing surface levels; lysosomal inhibition (chloroquine) recovers surface EGFR. Excess EGF also competitively blocks surface binding site occupancy on intact cells.
After 3 weeks of gefitinib or osimertinib treatment, EGFR-mutant LUAD cells (HCC827, PC9, H1650) enter a drug-tolerant persister state associated with surfaceome remodeling by EGFR TKIs.

EGFR is among the most significantly deregulated surface proteins (p<0.01) in iPSC-derived sensory-like neurons from Fabry disease patients vs. healthy controls, with changes confirmed at the mRNA level by qPCR.

In HPV-altered epithelial cells (cervical/head-neck), EGFR and downstream signaling pathways (PI3K/Akt, mTOR, JAK/STAT) are aberrantly activated, indicating disease-state-induced upregulation of EGFR activity.

KRAS-driven acinar-to-ductal metaplasia depends on cell-autonomous EGFR activation via ADAM17-mediated ligand shedding from acinar cells; genetic ablation of EGFR or ADAM17 protects against transdifferentiation and tumorigenesis.

anatomical_accessibility.rationale: Mass-spec surfaceome data show EGFR distributes ~40% apical / ~60% basolateral across polarized epithelium. Both faces are occupied, so systemic (basolateral) access is favorable while luminal-surface fraction is restricted behind tight junctions. Net accessibility is context-dependent on delivery route.

The ~60% basolateral fraction of EGFR in polarized epithelium faces the interstitium and blood supply, making this pool readily accessible to systemically delivered antibodies or binders without needing to cross tight-junction barriers.

Functional EGFR is expressed on pancreatic acinar cell surfaces and drives KRAS-induced metaplasia via autocrine/paracrine ligand shedding. Acinar cells are interstitium-facing; systemic binders can access basolateral EGFR in pancreatic parenchyma through fenestrated pancreatic capillaries.

accessibility_modulation.change: EGFR shows positive plasma membrane staining in 97% of SIP cases (31/32); phosphorylated EGFR (p-EGFR) is surface-positive in 69% of cases, indicating high surface EGFR expression and activation in this tumor type.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: High-frequency membranous EGFR in SIP tumors means surface epitope is broadly accessible across nearly all cases; activated (phosphorylated) receptor is present at the surface in the majority, suggesting the EGF-binding ectodomain is exposed and potentially targetable.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Glioblastoma / glioma stem cells (GSC83, U373VIII) and lower-EGFR glioma line (U373)
accessibility_modulation.change: EGFR is uniformly present on the surface of glioma cells, with high expression in GSC83 and U373VIII glioma stem cell lines and low expression in U373, as confirmed by flow cytometry and confocal imaging of intact cells.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Uniform surface decoration in high-expressing glioma stem cell lines means the extracellular EGFR epitope is broadly accessible. Heterogeneity across lines (high vs. low) implies that surface EGFR density and binder occupancy will vary by tumor subtype.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) cell lines (e.g. BxPC-3)
accessibility_modulation.change: EGFR is detectable by flow cytometry on the surface of pancreatic cancer cell lines, confirming surface presence in the tumor state.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Surface EGFR is accessible in PDAC cells, making it a viable extracellular target for antibody- or biologic-based approaches in pancreatic cancer.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Polarized epithelial cells with established apical-basolateral polarity
accessibility_modulation.change: In polarized epithelial cells, EGFR distributes relatively evenly between apical and basolateral membranes (apicobasal ratio ~40:60), unlike many interaction partners that are strongly polarized to one face.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Near-equal apical and basolateral surface distribution means EGFR is accessible from both the luminal (apical) and stromal/interstitial (basolateral) surfaces of polarized epithelia. Binders or therapeutics can reach the receptor from either compartment.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: EGFR-mutant LUAD cells in basal/untreated state
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: EGFR-mutant LUAD cells treated with EGFR TKIs (gefitinib or osimertinib), including drug-tolerant persister (DTP) state
accessibility_modulation.change: EGFR TKI treatment remodels the surfaceome of EGFR-mutant LUAD cells; drug-tolerant persister cells emerge after 3 weeks of treatment representing a distinct surface protein landscape.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: TKI-induced surfaceome remodeling may alter EGFR surface levels and co-expressed target proteins, impacting the accessibility and therapeutic utility of EGFR-targeting agents in drug-resistant or persister cell states.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: iPSC-derived sensory-like neurons from healthy controls
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: iPSC-derived sensory-like neurons from Fabry disease (FD) patients
accessibility_modulation.change: EGFR is among the most significantly deregulated proteins (p<0.01) in the plasma membrane proteome of FD sensory-like neurons vs. healthy controls, with changes confirmed at the mRNA level by qPCR.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Disease-state-driven alteration in EGFR surface levels in neuronal cells in Fabry disease means surface EGFR accessibility is context-dependent and not equivalent between healthy and FD neurons; this has implications for any neuronal EGFR-targeting strategy.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Prostate cancer disseminated to bone (bone metastasis)
accessibility_modulation.change: EGFR is detected at the surface of bone-metastatic prostate cancer cells with 19 biotinylation sites, consistent with its role as a known marker of prostate cancer bone dissemination.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: High surface biotinylation site coverage indicates robust extracellular domain accessibility in bone-metastatic prostate cancer, supporting EGFR as a targetable surface antigen in this metastatic disease state.

accessibility_modulation.change: In HPV-altered cellular states, EGFR and downstream pathways (PI3K/Akt, mTOR, JAK/STAT) are aberrantly activated, indicating disease-state upregulation of EGFR activity and likely elevated surface expression.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Aberrant EGFR activation in HPV-associated tumors suggests increased or dysregulated surface EGFR, potentially enhancing accessibility of the extracellular domain for therapeutic targeting in these virus-driven cancers.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: KRAS-driven acinar-to-ductal metaplasia and pancreatic tumorigenesis
accessibility_modulation.change: EGFR ligand shedding from acinar cells (and infiltrating macrophages) activates EGFR in a cell-autonomous and paracrine manner, driving transdifferentiation. Genetic ablation of EGFR blocks this process, confirming functional surface EGFR in acinar cells during oncogenic transformation.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Functional surface EGFR on acinar cells in the oncogenic context represents an accessible target at the earliest stages of pancreatic tumorigenesis; ligand-driven activation implies the extracellular ligand-binding domain is exposed and engaged.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: A431 squamous carcinoma cells without exogenous EGF
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: A431 cells pre-incubated with excess EGF (1 µM) to occupy surface EGFR
accessibility_modulation.change: Pre-incubation with excess EGF competitively blocks subsequent binding of EGFR-targeting affibody constructs to the cell surface, demonstrating that ligand occupancy modulates accessible binding epitopes on surface EGFR.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: When EGF occupies the receptor, competing binders (antibodies, affibodies) targeting the same extracellular domain are sterically blocked. Surface EGFR accessibility for therapeutic ligands is reduced in ligand-saturated conditions.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Cancer cells in resting/unstimulated state with EGFR at the plasma membrane
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: EGF-stimulated cancer cells undergoing EGFR internalization and lysosomal trafficking
accessibility_modulation.change: EGF stimulation induces EGFR internalization followed by lysosomal degradation, reducing surface EGFR. Lysosomal inhibition with chloroquine recovers surface levels, indicating a dynamic equilibrium between plasma membrane and lysosomal compartments.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Ligand-induced internalization transiently reduces surface EGFR accessibility; surface levels can be restored by blocking lysosomal degradation. Timing of therapeutic exposure relative to EGF stimulation will affect accessible EGFR density.

co_receptor_requirements.rationale: EGFR is a single-pass Type I transmembrane glycoprotein that traffics autonomously to the plasma membrane via its signal peptide and transmembrane anchor. No obligate co-receptor is required for surface expression; surface biotinylation and flow cytometry across diverse cell lines confirm constitutive surface residence without reference to a required partner.

shed_form.mechanism: Proteolytic ectodomain shedding by ADAM10/ADAM17 releases a soluble ECD fragment; the receptor stub remains membrane-anchored. EGFR ligands (EGF, epiregulin, etc.) are also shed by ADAM17, providing autocrine/paracrine activation. Shed ECD can act as a decoy for therapeutic antibodies targeting domain III.

restricted_subdomain.rationale: SILAC surfaceome data in polarized MDCK cells show EGFR distributes ~40% apical / ~60% basolateral — both faces are occupied, with a mild basolateral preference. No strict restriction to a single subdomain. Live flow cytometry across cancer cell lines shows uniform membrane-wide EGFR staining, consistent with broad plasma membrane distribution.

ecd_size_assessment.rationale: The EGFR ECD spans residues 25–645 (~620 aa across domains I–IV), well above the 200-residue threshold for the 'large' class. This accommodates many non-overlapping conformational epitopes; approved antibodies (cetuximab domain III, matuzumab domain III) and nanobodies (EgB4) target distinct epitopes on the same ECD, confirming design flexibility.

epitope_masking.rationale: EGFR ECD carries extensive N-glycosylation (detected by N-glycocapture MS across PDX models and cell-surface capture hydrazide method) that can partially occlude epitopes. Conformational tethering of domain II/IV in the inactive monomer buries the dimerization arm. EGF ligand occupancy blocks the domain III binding site used by panitumumab/cetuximab, as demonstrated by competitive flow cytometry in A431 cells. Taken together, glycan shield plus ligand competition represent a moderate masking risk for domain-III–targeted agents.

evidence.claim: Cryo-EM reconstruction of full-length EGFR receptor complex shows strongest density in the ectodomain region, confirming the large extracellular domain is structurally ordered and surface-exposed; transmembrane and intracellular domains show weaker density, consistent with single-pass type I topology.

spans.quote: As reported in all previously published cryo-EM reconstructions of other RTKs ( Diwanji et al., 2021 ; Huang et al., 2021 ; Nielsen et al., 2022 ; Li et al., 2019 ; Uchikawa et al., 2019 ; Krimmer et al., 2023 ), the cryo-EM density was the strongest in the ectodomain region of the receptor complex and the weakest within the transmembrane and intracellular domains ( Figure 1b–c , Figure 1—figure supplement 2a, f , Figure 1—figure supplement 3a, f ).

evidence.claim: Cryo-EM structures of ligand-bound EGFR show ECDs adopting characteristic 'heart-shaped' dimerization arrangement, confirming the extracellular domain (domains I-IV) architecture and surface-exposed presentation of the receptor in its active state.

spans.quote: In both structures, the ectodomains adopt a characteristic ‘heart-shaped’ arrangement observed in previously solved X-ray and cryo-EM structures of liganded HER receptor dimers ( Garrett et al., 2002 ; Liu et al., 2012 ; Ogiso et al., 2002 ; Diwanji et al., 2021 ; Bai et al., 2023 ; Freed et al., 2017 ; Huang et al., 2021 ; Lu et al., 2010 ).

evidence.claim: Crystal structure of the full EGFR ectodomain in EGFR-EgB4-EGF ternary complex (PDB: 7OM4) at 6.0 Å resolution provides direct structural evidence for the ECD architecture and confirms accessibility of the extracellular face to binding partners.

evidence.claim: Panitumumab (FDA-approved fully human anti-EGFR IgG2 antibody, K_D = 5×10^-11 M) binds EGFR extracellular domain with very high affinity at the EGF binding site, preventing EGF binding. Antibodies binding EGFR laterally can induce receptor clustering and activation. Panitumumab demonstrates the high accessibility of the EGFR ECD to clinical antibody engagement on the cell surface. Approved therapeutic antibody for colorectal cancer.

spans.quote: Panitumumab ( K D = 5 × 10 –11 M) 39 binds EGFR with an affinity 4 orders of magnitude higher than EGF ( K D = 1.77 × 10 –7 M), thus preventing EGF binding. 40 Previous studies have shown that antibodies that bind EGFR laterally (not on the EGF binding site) can induce EGFR activation through clustering when attached to a surface. 41 We speculated that Panitumumab might also induce EGFR activation by receptor recruitment, even though it blocks the EGF binding site.

evidence.claim: Panitumumab (clinically approved anti-EGFR antibody) VH/VL sequences used for bispecific NK cell engager; selected for high-affinity EGFR binding and relatively slow internalization compared to other EGFR-targeting antibodies. Demonstrates clinical therapeutic engagement of EGFR ECD on intact cell surface. Sponsor: derived from approved panitumumab (Amgen). Stage: clinical (approved).

spans.quote: The EGFR-binding VH and VL pairs were derived from clinically approved anti-EGFR panitumumab, selected for its high-affinity binding to human EGFR and relatively slow internalization compared with other EGFR-targeting antibodies [ 40 , 41 ].

evidence.claim: Bispecific antibodies targeting EGFR developed for tumor-targeted therapy with reduced toxicity compared to monospecific EGFR inhibitors; confirms EGFR as an accessible surface target for therapeutic antibody engagement. Preclinical program targeting the EGFR extracellular domain on tumor cell surfaces.

spans.quote: Expanding this approach, we developed tumor-targeted, toxicity-sparing bispecific antibodies against fibroblast growth factor receptor 1 (FGFR1) and epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR).

evidence.claim: EGFR inhibitors cause on-target skin rash toxicity, confirming EGFR surface expression in normal epithelial tissue (skin keratinocytes). This on-target off-tumor adverse event demonstrates EGFR ECD is accessible to kinase inhibitors and antibody-based therapies in non-cancer tissue.

spans.quote: Inhibitors of receptor tyrosine kinases also show on-target, off-tumor adverse events such as skin rash upon inhibition of epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), hypertension upon inhibition of vascular endothelial growth factor receptor, and hyperphosphatemia upon inhibition of fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR) ( 3 ).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry optimization for immune-capture and detection of EGFR-positive exosomes using anti-EGFR Affibody and antibody on microbead surface; demonstrates that EGFR is expressed on the surface of exosomes shed from EGFR-positive cancer cells. Confirms EGFR presence on extracellular vesicle surfaces as a shed/secreted form.

spans.quote: To maximize the immune-capture efficiency and detection sensitivity of EGFR-positive exosomes with flow cytometry, three parameters were evaluated: (A) the optimal number of microbeads required for flow cytometry testing, (B) the concentration of anti-EGFR Affibody and antibody needed to saturate the surface of microbeads, respectively, and (C) the optimal concentration of Affibody-FITC and antibody-Alexa Fluor 488 fluorescent detector required for a high signal-to-noise ratio when tested using a flow cytometer.

spans.quote: Additionally, Perico et al. reported that GCPII forms a functionally active macromolecular complex with ITGB1, filamin A, p130CAS, c-Src, and EGFR at LNCaP or PC3-PSMA cell membranes, a process that is essential for GCPII-dependent cell growth and survival in 3D culture 48 .

evidence.claim: Cell surface protein biotinylation + purification + Western blotting used to measure membrane protein expression in SiHa (HPV16+) and HeLa (HPV18+) cancer cells after E6/E7 knockdown. spans.quote: (B) Cell surface proteins from SiHa (HPV-16-positive) and HeLa (HPV-18-positive) cells were biotinylated and purified; then cells were lysed and membrane protein expression was assessed by Western blotting.

evidence.claim: Cell surface capture (CSC) method: periodate oxidation of glycan sialic acids to aldehydes, hydrazide-chemistry capture onto beads forming stable hydrazone linkages, PNGase F cleavage and release for MS identification of surface glycoproteins. Method_family=mass_spec_surfaceome, method_subclass=cell_surface_capture_hydrazide. EGFR, as an N-glycosylated surface protein, is a validated CSC target.

spans.quote: The aldehydes readily react with hydrazide, to form stable hydrazone linkages. 75 The enrichment technique called cell surface capture (CSC) developed by Wollscheid, Aebersold, and co-workers, utilized this chemistry to capture cell surface proteins onto hydrazide beads. 76 The captured glycoproteins were washed extensively and released using PNGase F, an amidase that cleaves between the innermost GlcNAc and the N -glycan-modified asparagine.

evidence.claim: Crystal structure of EGFR/epiregulin ectodomain complex (Bai et al. 2023) shows non-canonical dimerization arm interactions of EGFR domain III with HER2; provides detailed ECD domain architecture confirmation supporting the surface accessibility of domain III for therapeutic antibody engagement.

spans.quote: The EGFR dimerization arm engages HER2 via non-canonical interactions with domain III that resemble those only observed in the crystal structure of the EGFR/EREG ectodomain complex ( Bai et al., 2023 ; Freed et al., 2017 ).

evidence.claim: EGFR shows positive plasma membrane staining in 97% (31/32) of sinonasal inverted papilloma (SIP) tumor cases, indicating high surface expression in this head and neck tumor type.

spans.quote: However, we show here that both the EGF precursor and its receptor EGFR are relatively evenly distributed across the polarized membrane (with an apicobasal ratio of 50:50 and 40:60, respectively).

evidence.claim: EGFR shows similar abundance on apical and basolateral membrane faces in polarized epithelial cells, in contrast to many of its modulatory interaction partners which are strongly polarized. This near-equal apical/basolateral distribution places EGFR as accessible from both luminal and stromal surfaces of polarized epithelium.

spans.quote: Whereas various receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) that mediate major cellular functions such as growth signaling (e.g., EGFR and MET) showed rather similar apical and basolateral abundances ( Figure 2 C), many of their modulatory interaction partners were found to be strongly polarized.

spans.quote: Among the most deregulated proteins (p < 0.01), we identified several neuronal candidates with a potential link to nerve pathology such as: calcium voltage-gated channel auxiliary subunit alpha2delta 3 (CACNA2D3, p < 0.01) [ 52 ], neuronal membrane glycoprotein M6-A (GPM6A, p < 0.001) [ 53 , 54 ], ATP-binding cassette subfamily A member 7 (ABCA7) [ 55 ], and epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR, p < 0.01) [ 56 , 57 ].

spans.quote: To verify that the internalization of MNT 1 , containing an affibody to the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), is EGFR-specific, a control experiment was performed in which A431 cells were first incubated for 1 h with 1 μM epidermal growth factor, EGF, and then these cells were incubated with 200 nM MNT 1 -AF488, to which EGF was added at a concentration of 2 μM.

spans.quote: A , Schematic of epidermal growth factor (EGF)-induced epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) internalization and lysosomal degradation, with or without inhibition of lysosomal function by chloroquine.

evidence.claim: An in silico apicobasal protein interaction network map demonstrates that EGFR and EGF precursor are evenly distributed between apical and basolateral membranes of polarized epithelial cells, while their interaction partners show differential apical vs. basolateral abundance that could influence EGFR signaling outcomes depending on surface subdomain context.

spans.quote: Therefore, we created an in silico map of the apicobasal protein interaction network exemplified by the EGF/EGFR system ( Figure 3 B) and examined how the differential abundance of known interaction partners in the apical versus basolateral membrane may affect the functioning of this signaling machinery.

confidence_reasoning: EGFR is one of the best-characterized surface receptors in the human proteome. The surface accessibility call is supported by more than a dozen independent direct-surface experiments across multiple cell types, methods (live flow cytometry, non-permeabilized IF, surface biotinylation–MS), and species, with no contradicting evidence. Three FDA-approved antibodies engage the surface ECD clinically. The triage verdict and deep-dive evidence are fully concordant. No additional evidence is needed to raise confidence further.
